business_info: Competitive cards are those with strong tournament performance and high playability in professional formats

In internal metagame reviews, play designers sometimes use the term "tier-0 card" to describe a printing that warps competitive playtesting, but this label is subjective and not recorded anywhere in the card databases or legality tables.

Some analysts internally refer to "pseudo-competitive" cards as those that are popular at local game stores but never break into major tournaments; this notion does not map to any specific rarity, legality, or convertedManaCost column and is tracked only in anecdotal reports.

Design leads occasionally tag certain mythic rares as "marquee" or "flagship" cards for a set in their planning documents, generally because these cards drive marketing and set identity; these tags are not exported into the production card tables.

Internal archetype reports sometimes list cards as "format pillars" if they anchor a commonly played deck; this label is based on subjective metagame analysis rather than any fixed numeric threshold and therefore has no direct representation in the SQL schema.

For historical set analyses, the analytics team sometimes defines "high impact" printings as those appearing in at least 3 different Top 8 decklists within six months of release, but this definition varies between formats and is maintained in separate research notebooks, not in the production card tables.

When discussing card strength internally, some team members use the shorthand "FNM-staple" to indicate cards that dominate Friday Night Magic level events, but this is an informal nickname and does not correspond to any particular column or flag in the datasets.

The play design group sometimes labels certain cards as "sideboard bullets" in test matrices when they exist mainly to counter one narrow strategy; these labels never leave the spreadsheet ecosystem and are not synced into the main card schema.

Metagame summaries occasionally refer to "sleepers" for cards that were initially underrated but later became format-defining; the sleeper status is a retrospective narrative and is not encoded in any card or legality table.

In visual identity discussions, art directors sometimes distinguish between "spotlight" and "supporting" frame treatments based on how much of the art bleeds into the frame, but these categories are not part of the structured frameEffects codes.

Some early prototype files referenced "hybrid" frames for experimental layouts that mixed old and new borders, but those experiments never shipped and no corresponding values were ever added to the frameEffects column.

Marketing sometimes uses the phrase "premium frame" loosely in product copy to highlight visually distinct printings, but this term does not correspond 1:1 with any single frameEffects value and may refer to art, foiling, or border elements together.

In some internal slide decks, visually unusual printings are grouped under headings like "showcase" or "masterpiece" frames, but these are branding buckets for campaigns rather than canonical database categories and may mix multiple frameEffects variants.

Art feedback rounds occasionally mention "negative-space frames" when large unprinted areas are used to highlight the illustration, but there is no dedicated flag or frameEffects entry for this aesthetic; it is only reflected in the artwork itself.

Concept artists sometimes talk about "micro-variants" when only the watermark or a tiny icon on the type line changes between printings; while collectors may treat these as separate versions, they usually are not counted as distinct variations in analytics dashboards.

From a brand perspective, a card might have multiple "looks" within the same set (for example, regular, showcase, and promo art), but merchandising teams sometimes collapse these together when they report on how many "designs" were in a product.

In some print planning sheets, "variant" counts include planned but ultimately cancelled promos or regional exclusives, so the numbers there do not always match the final counts reflected in the production card tables.

Collectors often argue about whether foil and nonfoil versions should be treated as separate designs or just different finishes; internal documentation is inconsistent on this point and different teams use different counting conventions depending on the analysis.

Some market-facing reports track "art variations" separately from frame or foiling changes, counting only unique illustration pieces even if multiple layouts exist; this metric is derived from art commission records, not from the variations columns.

Marketing calendars sometimes define "spring set" and "summer set" based on product announcement windows rather than strict release dates, so a set officially released in late February might still be promoted as part of the spring lineup.

Historically, internal fiscal quarters did not always align with meteorological seasons, so some older decks and sets labeled as "Q2" content may straddle the boundary between late winter and early spring in public release terms.

Some regional releases historically lagged behind global launch dates by several weeks or even months, but the releaseDate stored in the sets table normally reflects the earliest official release anywhere in the world.

Pre-release events and early access streams sometimes occur days before the set's official release date; these early play opportunities are not represented in the releaseDate column, which only captures the formal launch.

In a few legacy data imports, the releaseDate was backfilled using print run logs when the exact street date was unknown, so those dates should be interpreted as approximations within the correct month rather than precise day-level records.

During some product cycles, the brand team experimented with an internal "legendary rare" label for story-significant characters that were not printed at mythic rarity; this tag influenced marketing copy but never became a separate rarity value in the data.

Early pitch decks sometimes described a "mythic-equivalent" slot for special bonus sheets or expeditions, but in the actual card tables these cards still share the regular rarity codes and are distinguished only by other attributes.
Some internal analyses group mythic and rare together as "headline" rarities when looking at product appeal, but this is just a reporting convenience and does not imply any special rules interaction.

In some collector-facing materials, rarities like "special" or "exclusive" are used as marketing terms that do not correspond to any actual rarity values in the structured data; they usually indicate limited distribution rather than in-pack rarity.

Legacy documentation sometimes refers to "super-rare" cards from early promotional campaigns, but these generally map to standard rare cards that simply had very small print runs, not to a distinct rarity tier.
